g.data-narration: Welcome to the Exponential Distribution - a fundamental continuous probability distribution with wide applications.
The exponential distribution models the time between consecutive events in a Poisson process - continuous and independent events at constant rate.
g.text: Models the waiting time between events in a Poisson process.
Events occur continuously and independently at a constant average rate λ.

g.data-narration: The distribution has key properties: continuous values starting at zero, defined by rate parameter λ, and the unique memoryless property.
The probability density function f(x) equals λe^(-λx) for x≥0. The rate parameter λ controls how quickly probability decays.
g.text: If X ~ Exp(λ), then the Probability Density Function is:

g.data-narration: To verify this is a valid PDF, we confirm f(x)≥0 always, and integrate from zero to infinity to get exactly 1.
g.text: Verifying f(x) is a Valid PDF

g.data-narration: The cumulative distribution function F(x) gives P(X≤x) = 1 - e^(-λx). The survival function is its complement: e^(-λx).
The expected value E(X) equals 1/λ - the inverse of the rate. The variance equals 1/λ², making standard deviation also 1/λ.
The standard deviation equals the mean at 1/λ. The Moment Generating Function M(t) = λ/(λ-t) for t less than λ.
g.text: MGF: MX(t) = λ/(λ - t), t < λ

g.data-narration: The memoryless property states: given survival for time s, the probability of surviving t more is the same as starting fresh.
g.text: The exponential is the ONLY continuous distribution with this property:
P(X > s + t | X > s) = P(X > t)

g.data-narration: The proof uses conditional probability: P(A|B) = P(A∩B)/P(B). Since X>s+t implies X>s, we simplify to e^(-λt).
Exponential and Poisson are intimately connected: Poisson counts events in an interval; exponential measures time between consecutive events.
g.text: Poisson: Counts events in time t

g.data-narration: Example: Mean inter-arrival of 5 minutes gives λ=0.2. Probability of arrival within 3 minutes is 1-e^(-0.6) = 0.4512.
g.text: Mean=5 min → λ=1/5=0.2 per min. Find P(X≤3):

g.data-narration: Example: Bulb mean 1000 hours. Given 500 hours survived, probability of 300 more hours uses memoryless: e^(-0.3) = 0.7408.
g.text: Bulb mean=1000h. Already lasted 500h. Find P(last 300 more | survived 500):

g.data-narration: Key applications: queueing theory, reliability engineering, network traffic, radioactive decay, and survival analysis.
Remember: λ = 1/mean. Use CDF for probabilities. Apply memoryless property when given past survival time. Practice derivations!
